Ladies and gentlemen, we have the great David Deutsch with us today. I have a lot of admiration for David and his work. David is one of the greats of copywriting. I'm very, very, very, very privileged to introduce him. Let me just share with you a little bit about David. David has worked with the great David Oglevy. He started at Oglevy and Martha on Medicine Avenue. He's worked with clients such as Merrill Lynch, General Food, American Express. He has written a gazillion direct response ads for people like Agora, Boardroom, tons of controls. He now has a published author. Basically, let's just put it this way. David is one of the people, if you start on copywriting, sooner or later you hear his name and everyone has a lot of reference for what he has done and also for the lessons that he has taught. So David is truly one of the greats of copywriting. The Heddy Gell, her name is probably Long Dawn On, and she counted David as one of the mentors and one of the folks that she looked up to and led herself, a very good copywriter. So David is the copywriter's mentor. So welcome to the show, David. Very, very happy you're on. And I can't wait for what you have to say also. Well, great. It's great to be here. I'm honored. I'm flattered by the introduction. It's great to listen to. I didn't want it to stop. David, I have a very simple question because a lot of the folks who are listening, maybe they don't aspire to be copywriters. I think they are coaches, consultants. They are some of them are marketers. Some of them are financial people or real estate people and some of them are service providers, but all of them have one problem right now. The internet has made competition very stiff. They wish to stand out from other folks. And maybe they don't want to be a copywriter. Maybe they think copywriting and marketing is too difficult. But what will you say are some strategies that ordinary folks like themselves can stand out in a competitive market? Well, I think there's two things there. One is it's you don't have to be a copywriter, right? It's kind of a false dichotomy to say someone has to be a copywriter to write copy. I think any business owner should be able and it's an amazing competitive advantage to be able to write good copy about what you do. You know, I work with a lot of people, a lot of business owners. They know their business. They know the market, right? They have this advantage in writing copy. And just by learning a few simple things about writing copy, they're able to write the best, better copy for their business than any copywriter could possibly write. So to be able to write good, you know, advertising copy, social media copy. There's so many places now where writing is such a critical advantage, right? For, for getting business, for separating yourself from the competition. You know, even if you hire writers, if you know, if you're able to write, if you know what good writing is, that gives you an advantage in hiring writers. So I don't see why every, you know, owner of a business, consulting business or otherwise, you know, wouldn't learn to write as well as they can. I see. So I think let's address also one of the concerns that folks have. So some, sometimes folks think, I am not a very good writer or, you know, I'm not very good with English. It's a copywriter made or they born or they realize, you know, maybe I'm not very vocal, I'm not very good at marketing. Can you learn copywriting or is it something you're born with? What are your thoughts? Yeah, well, it's certainly you're born with some of the elements that go into making a good copywriter, you know, whether that's intelligence, curiosity, you know, an understanding of people, maybe not born with an understanding of people, but that's something that you need. But it's definitely a learnable skill. And, you know, I think it's just it's getting over some of the negatives, like I'm not good with English or something like that. Some of the best writers I know are people who, for whom English isn't their second language, isn't their first language. Eduardo Albuquerque, most successful, you know, writer at Agora right now, you know, in I think, or at least in Agora Financial, English is not his, you know, first language. So there's a lot of people and you probably know a lot of them because it's almost an advantage because it forces you to write simply. It forces you to understand writing and to analyze it and to deconstruct it. And to learn it, you know, in a different way. So I wouldn't let that stop you. I wouldn't let anything like I'm not a writer or I'm not able to write. If you can talk, you can write. You can sell your services. You can talk about yourself one-on-one to someone. Then you can put that on paper. But when people sit down to write, you know, or to write like this, they, they get into this writing frame of mind, right? It's like they're back in school and they're writing a school paper. And so it comes out stilted. It comes out very academic sounding, comes out kind of, you know, you know, awkward instead of just talking one-on-one to the prospect. And that's what I train people to do. I don't really train people to write so much as I train people to kind of access that part in their brain that knows how to persuade and get words down on paper that they know is how to persuade someone. So I guess the most logical question to ask Nick is how do you persuade someone, especially in print? What exactly makes a argument or a case or in this case, a sales letter persuasive? Well, you know, I think one of the things is just, you know, one piece of advice I give people is to study what makes a good court case, study how lawyers win cases, right? There's a book by Jerry Spence, how to win every case or something like that. But it's a book about, it's a great book in how to convince people of things, how to persuade people. He's trying to persuade a jury, but we go through the same things to persuade people, right? We, we, you have to make them like you and trust you first of all, right? Even if I want to do business with you, if I don't trust you, I'm not going to do business with you, right? You could be selling me $10 bills for $5, but if I don't trust you, I'm, I'm not going to buy those $10 bills from you for $5. Wow. So, yeah. So you have to start with that, right? And then you have to get their attention, right? You have to tell them things they don't know. You have to tell them things that make them go, wow, you know, you have to tell them things that make them question maybe things that they believe in order to get them to believe that you have the solution now. You know, you've got to get them to believe that, that there's a reason that they're suffering from this problem, right? There's the reason they're not making money. It's a reason they're unhealthy. And it's a reason they hadn't considered, right? And then you've got the solution for that, right? There's a reason that you're not able to write great copy. And that's that you're not, you're not accessing that inner part of you that's able to write great copy. And I have the solution for that. I will show you how to access that inner part of you that, that is able to write great copy. So that's, that's an example of how I might do a sales pitch, right? And how I might persuade. Hmm. I think that is a, that is, that is highly a very unique angle because, because I, I think that is providing them with a new perspective that they have never considered before and therefore providing a solution to this new perspective. So you're actually in the business of selling ideas, which is, you know, they had, they thought that they had a problem because of this old, this old thing. But you sell, they let them know, no, your real problems, the cause of your real problems, this thing. And here's a solution for this. Is that what? Yeah. Just have to be careful not to move, not to try to move them too far, right? Cause people believe what they believe and you can't convince them that, you know, this politician is better than that politician. You can't move them too far off their beliefs, right? But you can just sort of open their eyes to things they haven't considered before. Like the classic thing is with weight loss, right? People for years were like, we'll show you how to lose 20 pounds. We'll show you how to do this. We'll show you how to lose a lot of weight. And then someone came along and said, you know, the reason you're not able to lose weight is because your hormones are out of whack. Your hormones control how hungry you are, your hormones control how fast you metabolize your hormones control every aspect of your body. So that's why you haven't been able to lose weight because you haven't gotten your hormones under control. We will show you how to get your hormones under control and how to lose weight, you know, through doing that. So that's a good example. It's not like changing some whole belief about that they have about something. It's just kind of opening their eyes to a different way of looking at it. Like, Hey, maybe you're, maybe your inability to lose weight is caused by hormones. Let us show you that and let us help you lose weight by balancing your hormones now. And David, I was just curious. So how did you go about, go about finding these new perspectives or like these new things to open up their minds? Or how, how would you even go about, or is there any project or case study that you've worked on before that stood out to you the most that you managed to convince something that's supposedly very hard to, to shift or to? Well, I think, you know, it just takes a lot of digging, right? It really, because if you ask a client, what's the thing, right? That that's different, whatever the client, a lot of times isn't able to articulate that. And if you're the client, sometimes you can't articulate that about what you do, your, your own consulting or your own business. It's hard. It's hard for me to do that for my own business, right? So you've got to, you've got to really ask a lot of questions, right? Of yourself, of your client and really kind of get to what do I do that's different, right? What do I, you know, what do I do that maybe no one else is doing? What piece of what I do can kind of be highlighted and brought out? So for that hormone thing, you know, maybe the client didn't really realize how important that was. They were like, I don't know, my pill just helps you lose weight by, you know, it just makes your hormone, you know, makes your metabolism, you know, faster. Same thing everyone else's pill does. Well, yeah, but how does it make your, how does it make your metabolism go faster? Well, you know, well, there are hormones that, you know, balance your metabolism that, that speed it up or slow it down. There are hormones even that make you hungry or not hungry. And, you know, that's, that's how you can control your weight or without pill. And so you realize, wow, that's like gold. That's, you mean, it's hormones that are doing this, right? And the client goes, yeah, well, I guess I thought everybody knew that, but they don't. They, you get, what is it called when you're inside something, you're a fish and you don't know you're in water, right? They don't know what's cool about what they do, what's interesting, what's new, what's different. So you got to just dig. You got to really be relentless, relentless about that. Even with yourself, like, what do I do? What do I do that's different? How is my coaching different? How is my, you know, product different? What aspect of it is different? And then that kind of loops around to when you're developing products or you're developing your services, maybe think about offering things that are different. Think about building that into. Your product or what you offer. Wow. Wow. How, how important is research and how important is the headline in a copy? I know this is a two, like, you know, not exactly, you know, one after the other, but how important is research? Let's just talk, never talk about research show. How important is it and what do you do with the research? Cause you just mentioned the idea of like, you know, asking questions and digging. You know, research is, it sounds a little cliche, but it's hugely important, right? Because it's hard to be a better writer, right? In the purely writing sense, right? It's hard to be like Hemingway. It's hard to be, get a lot better at that. There are certain things you can do and be a better writer, but anyone can be a better researcher. And research is what powers better copywriting, not writing, but more persuasive copywriting. Right. So the writing could be at the same level, but it's more powerful because there's research behind it. You know, you read something sometimes and you read it and you go, you know, boy, it's obviously written by a copywriter. And, and sometimes you read something and you go, wow, this sounds like it was written by the doctor himself. This sounds like it was written by someone that really, I'm in good hands here. I feel like this person really knows their subject. And it's so powerful when a writer, you know, I could tell copy right away where the writer just has a superficial understanding or has an in depth understanding. Right. If you're writing about weight loss, you should know all about weight loss. Right. You should know how people lose weight. What does it mean to lose weight? Where does the weight go when you lose weight? How does that happen? How is fat broken down? Right. What are the different types of diets out there? How do people feel when they're, and, you know, and the psychological aspect, that's the technical stuff, right? The science. Right. You got to know that. Then you got to know, like people's attitudes about weight loss. Like it was a big revelation for me to find out that people who are overweight are kind of embarrassed about being overweight. So they're always there. A lot of them are kind of feeling like people are always watching them. People like if they're on an airplane, the person is kind of, you know, maybe looking at them like, Hey, you're taking up more of your seat than you should. Oh, or judging what they eat. Oh, wow. But if you think about it, that makes sense, right? Like, you know, whatever physically, you know, you are, if you're in places with other people who are not like that, you feel out of place. You feel a little bit embarrassed, right? And so when you understand that, you can talk to your audience differently. So, you know, you're maybe talking to people that want to want to make more money, let's say, and want to build their business and want to be more successful. Like, how do they feel about that? What's their motivation? Like, is their motivation really to make more money? Like, you know, your clients and your clients, clients, right? Is their motivation maybe to make more money or is it something else? Is it to prove themselves? Like, why do people want to make more money? Right? Money can only buy things up to a point, right? Up to 75,000 American dollars. People are no happier if they make more money. Right? And so, you know, money represents being able to take care of your family. Money represents being able to prove to your brother-in-law, hey, I'm, you know, I'm as successful as you are. It represents being able to prove to your fourth grade teacher that said you would never amount to anything. Hey, see, I did amount to something. And maybe your parents told you you'd never amount to anything. Or, you know, don't go into business for yourself. Go work for a company that's secure, you know, and you want to prove that you're successful. So if you, if you kind of know that stuff, that's research, right? If you know that stuff about the people you're talking to, you talk to them in a very different way. You don't just keep saying, hey, you can make more money. You can make more money. You say, hey, oh, like, what's it going to be like when you, you know, when you are able to take your family on that vacation to Disney World, you couldn't afford? What's it going to be like when you go to your class reunion and find your fourth grade teacher and say, hey, look, I'm, you know, I'm very successful now. What's your life going to be like? Wow. It sounds like you dig. So easy. See, I think this is a very unique, so we're going to do a lot of different folks. Things are very unique perspective on, on research, because it just means that the way you see research is to dive in deeper beyond the surface so that you, A, when it comes to the science and the logic of what you're talking about, especially if it's not like, you know, a topic you already know a lot about, you have a mastery and the fluidity about talking about it, that, that people can connect with. And also you understand the emotional, the actual deep emotional aspirations and context that the people aspire for and are suffering from. It seems like that's what you dive in. People just give that, you know, lip service. They just sort of, they know, yeah, yeah, yeah, I need to know the psychographics of my audience. I need to know their income. I need to know what their hot buttons are, but they don't really dig, right? And I feel like a lot of my work as a writer, trainer of writers is not so much to train people in writing. It's the light, keep pushing them to go deeper, keep pushing their head. No, no, go back in the cave and dig out more deep stuff, right? Go spend time with your prospects, right? Buy them a drink, get them drunk and find out what they really, you know, care about deep down, what their fears are, what keeps them up at night? Wow, this is a unique perspective on research. There's a lot of people who say, okay, cool, research is very important, but, but I think we have captured the idea that there is this, because yeah, a lot of, and, and I think this is so important, because we have a lot of so-called copy here, here in Asia and a lot of the copy is very superficial. You know, they always talk, as you said, you want to make more money, you want to be financially free, you want to retire early, you want to go on a holiday, they all vary, what do you call that, a surface level? Right. This skim the surface. They never ever dive deep within just to find out is this like all that people want. So I think that that is a very, very good point. My next question is how important is this concept of the big idea and how, how important is the headline in general? I think it depends on what you mean by a big idea, right? If, to me, a big idea is also to sell weight loss by selling a hormonal solution, right? That's a big idea. Um, to me, a big idea is, is to touch the underlying, you know, deeper, um, hot buttons of a prospect, you know, and if you can do that, if you can, like if you look at Dan Kennedy's writing, right? Dan Kennedy doesn't really have big ideas in a way. He just touches your emotional buttons. Yeah. Yeah. Exactly what to say. It doesn't have the agora type big ideas. Yeah. Yeah. You know, and certainly in the financial market, a big idea is kind of important because, you know, it's in that fifth stage of Schwartz, you know, awareness, right? People have seen it all. So you need something to break away from that. So in the financial market, the big idea is really important because you're just selling another newsletter that, you know, is going to get income for you. You're selling another newsletter about tech stocks or you're selling. So digging deeper in a way is, is helpful, but that's not what's going to get you the big breakthrough. What's going to get you the big breakthrough is the big idea of, you know, talking about something in a different way, you know, changing the way you talk about it, getting people's attention. You know, the, the, I always think of the Euler's, you know, the four-core horsemen of the coming financial apocalypse or something. Yeah. Oh my God. That's like a big headline kind of idea, right? The end of America was a big idea. How to rob banks was a great headline, right? Because it, you know, it, it drew you in. You had to find out more about that. Magic calendar, magic calculator was, were great ways of like talking about technical analysis, which is really boring subject, right? Like we'll show you how to read charts and you can make a lot of money when you read the chart and see when it goes up and it goes down and it goes up and it goes up again. That's when you buy, you know, and if the percentage of this is the percentage of that. And so it's like really boring stuff. But if you make it into a magic calculator, well, all you have to do is look at our magic calculator and you'll get, you know, we'll get all these great returns, then all of that big idea makes it so much easier to understand and, and to get people's like a magic calculator. Ooh, this sounds interesting. I have to read this. That is, that is be awesome. What, what makes a good headline? Like, like, like you've seen so many great headlines. We're just talking about this book here with all the, you know, great advertisements from before. What, what makes a good headline? You know, I mean, this, this book has all the, you know, they all laugh when I sat down at the piano, but when I started playing and, you know, you make this, this sticks in English, so many headline templates in your vast experience, you know, what, what makes a quality hit? Long shot templates or what do you say? Yeah, it could be long or short, right? It's just like Abraham Lincoln said about a man's legs. They should be long enough to reach the ground, right? That you want to, you want your headline to just, I mean, the whole purpose of the headline is to get them to read the next paragraph, right? No one looks at a headline and says, I'm buying this product, right? They have to read in order to find out about the product and want to buy it or your services and use you, right? No one's going to call you up and say, Hey, I want you, I want to consult. I want you to consult with my company just because of a headline. So all the headline has to do is get you to read the next paragraph, right? And it does that with, you know, by, with curiosity. It does that by providing you with, with something you didn't know before. It does that by providing you with, you know, with something that's, you know, that's new and different. It does that by providing you with something that shocks you a little bit. It does that by, you know, making, you know, by, you know, people don't like to be wrong about things, right? And if you tell someone, Hey, you think this is good for you, but I'm going to show you why it's not that kind, that gets your attention, right? Health foods that are bad for you. Oh, that is good. Yeah. Yeah. And, and, and Arthur Johnson, you know, got attention with a big idea years ago with like, you know, why you should drink more coffee and not drink so much what like just went in the opposite direction. Wow. Okay. Why coffee is good for you, right? Why things that you thought were bad are good for you. And of course that gets your attention. I mean, that really gets your attention because I want to eat all those things that are bad for me. Right. So, wow. Wow. That is, yeah, that is good stuff. You know, you got to, you got to wake people up. Right. People write headlines and they write copy thinking that people are like waiting to eagerly, you know, like devour what they've written. And they're not. They're waiting to answer their cell phone and, and text their friends and, and get on with their business and, and put out fires. And so you've got to, you can't just be, oh, this is important. Or, you know, same old, make lots of money, whatever. You've got to really, you know, grab them and, and pull them in. Right. With, with, and, and you see the way they laughed when I sat down at the piano, but when I started to play, does it still does that to this day? It kind of, you can feel the pull of that headline. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. I think we were talking about this, like, like how important is, is a story element within the copy, especially both in the headline and also in the story element. Like how important is story and what makes a good story? Well, you know, what makes a good story is, first of all, one of the advice I give people is study screenwriting, right? Study, study story, study heroes, read the hero's journey. Know what a story arc is, right? Story arc is like, starts in the ordinary world. There's, there's problem. There's conflict. It reaches, you know, it reaches a breaking point. And then the third act, you know, the conflict is resolved. And, you know, everybody lives happily ever after the hero comes back from his journey, bringing knowledge. The empire is staved in Star Wars, right? So a good story is a story that's got an arc to it. It's not like, you know, I went out, I learned these things. I made a lot of money. I'm really happy and I can buy all these things, right? That's a very flat story. But if it was like, you know, you know, my family was evicted and we were out on the street and I had to find a way to make money and I found someone that taught me these secrets and I had a struggle with my own self-doubts and I had a struggle with the first thing I tried to do failed. But then I realized that what I really needed was this element that I'm going to teach you about. And so then I was successful because I learned that success is about this. It's not about that. And so now I do all these great things and now I'm bringing this knowledge back. So that's got a little more of an arc to it. So you want to hear about people's struggles. You want to hear about how they, you know, the origin story, how they found out about things. You know, you want people doing interesting things. And that's, you know, like that woman with the necklace, right? The story. The story. Because, you know, oh, we didn't, something, there was a twist. We didn't expect something. They duplicated a necklace for her and they laid out both necklaces before her. And we think, OK, she'll just, you know, say, oh, nice copy. You know, it almost looks real. But no, she like, she picks up one and says, well, the resemblance is remarkable, but I could tell, you know, which one is mine. But it turns out that that's the duplicate. It was so good that she mistook the duplicate for the real necklace. So that's the story, right? But it's got movement to it. It's got an arc to it. And that's what you want, right? You want, you know, and sometimes a good headline takes you into the middle of a story. Right. Like the inquirer is great at that, right? There I was. My head was in the lion's mouth. I could feel his teeth about to sink into my neck, right? That they take you to the middle. No, the day started or ordinarily enough as we started out on our safari into the jungle. I never expected that, right? But it starts in the middle of the story, right? So sometimes you want to start in the middle of your story, right? You want to start in the middle of the pain that you went through or the uncertainty or winding up on the street or, you know, whatever it is. But don't you have to start in the beginning, right? Like, start in the middle of it where it's interesting and you get someone's attention. At some point in time, David, you know, you have to explain what your thing does and you transition away from the story. And I think that's when a lot of people struggle because, you know, you're right. You know, you either have to teach financial products or they have to explain about health and, you know, talk about arteries and red blood cells or, you know, they have to talk about whatever their system is and that's when that's usually the part of the copy that starts a flag and it gets boring and people get tuned out. How do people engage and actually like cover what the heck they do? Like, what are the steps and the process they do without just having people just, oh my God, this is the most boring thing. Well, there's two answers to that. One is get really good at explaining to people what you do without putting them to sleep or what your product does, right? So you start by, you know, talking to someone and you say, well, it's got R3 and the R3, you know, kills red blood cells and then red plumbers, you're going to be getting the person. It's like, you can see the person is like nodding out. They're not excited. So, okay, you try again, right? You say, maybe you make it simpler. Maybe you just keep doing that until you realize when people's eyes light up, right? You know, oh, you mean it, really? It makes my metabolism go faster. So I'll lose weight. That's pretty cool. Right. So, oh, okay. That's, I need to put that in my copy. So if you can't explain it to someone in an interesting way, you're not ready to sit down and write copy about it. Wow. Just keep talking to people about what you do and seeing what their reactions are, right? This is why salespeople get better and better because they're always getting feedback, right? They're always seeing the client get bored. They're always seeing the people look in their eye, right? As a writer, we can't do that. We don't see people when we're writing. So go out and see people. Go out and really talk to people about your product, about whatever it is. You know, talk to your partner about it, even, your wife, your husband. And, you know, get it so that they let them tell you what their reaction is, right? So that's one answer, right? Is be able to do that for God's sake. Because you can't write unless you can do that. You're using writing for thinking. You're using writing for working that out. And that's not what writing is for. Writing is for translating what you already know how to do in your head. You know, the pitch onto the page. So that's the first answer, right? Yeah. Second answer is that you've got to explain to people the mechanism, right? Is to really understand what a mechanism is, right? The thing, what is the thing that makes your thing do what it does, right? What is the thing that makes your coaching, your consulting, your being a doctor, your being a lawyer? What is the thing that you do that's unique that enables you to do what you do? And, you know, it's, you know, in the case of the weight loss thing that we keep coming back to, it's being able to manipulate the hormones, right? The hormones that make you hungry or make you feel full. The hormones that speed up your metabolism so you burn up all that fat. Even the hormones that make you happy because when you're happy, you don't eat too much and gain weight, right? Like we eat more when we're unhappy. So that's the mechanism. And it's pretty simple, right? You don't have to over complicate that. And so I think, and don't get into the explainer mode, right? I think when people write, that's why the pitching thing is so important in person. When people write, they're like, okay, now I got to explain this and they start, you know, like I got to tell them this in order to tell them this, then I have to explain this. And then I have to tell them to stop it. You know, you just just say it like you were talking to someone. Be interesting. Be selling all the time. Never take your eye off the fact that you're trying to get someone to take a certain action. You know, think about whether you really have to explain the exact details of how the hormone system works, like you're giving an anatomy lesson, right? Well, the hormone system works by, you know, taking micro doses of small, you know, you know, chemical compounds that activate things in your body and, you know, like don't do that. Just see that people are getting bored when you start doing that. And find another way to convey it without going into what I call lecture mode, right? Like don't sound like you're lecturing. Just sound like you're just explaining it to someone, like you would explain it to them, you know, across a table. Like you would explain it to your partner, you know, like, honey, this thing is really amazing, right? It works by activating your, your hormonal system. And that's what makes you lose weight because it makes, you know, hormones are what makes you be hungry or not hungry. Hormones are what makes you be happy and unhappy. Hormones are what speed up your metabolism. So you'll burn up all that fat. And then if you could say, let me explain to you how the hormone system works, honey, you know, then your partner would just go, you know, go away. I don't want to hear about how the hormone system works. Yeah. Yeah, I think at the end of the day, David, I think it goes back to what you're mentioning at the start. And, you know, I'm getting a good sense of what, of, of like the way you coach and the way you get people to write here. You're getting them to write away from what I call even a copywriter. So the worst kind of copywriter is the one where they sound like a copywriter and you're getting them to write like a human being. And I think that's, that's exactly like, like everything about this is getting people to move away from writing like a copywriter and writing like, like a normal freaking person. So they don't have too much copywriter wish or writer geese because sometimes we can tell when a person has had like, you know, way too much copywriting training and they are just giving you copyright and speak with everything. That's why it's, you know, it's almost a disadvantage to be good at writing. And, and, and it's almost you almost have to unlearn certain things, right? Because when someone's pretty good at writing, they can sort of make it sound like these things sound. They can sort of make it sound like it's supposed to sound like. But it doesn't have any real power to it. And people recognize that it sounds like, like these kinds of things. Doesn't have newness or freshness to it. Like when you read Gary Halbert's copy, right? Doesn't sound like copyright or copy. Sounds like Gary's just talking to you, you know? Yeah. First time I took Gary Halbert on the phone, I was like struck by the fact that he sounded like his copy. It was like I was talking to his copy. I don't really know how to describe it exactly. But he exactly like he sounds in his copy. And that's what made it so powerful, you know? Wow. Wow. Wow. So what's the meaning? So I, I'm just curious. What is it? I know this might sound like a circular question, probably a comment before, but, but it's one thing that, that so few copywriters are able to do, which is, you know, get all of these points in, have it all flow, do the right research, yet sound like a normal person. How the heck do you do that? You got to compile all of this research, you got to know what you're talking about. But yet you got to sound like a normal person without sound ring, like, you know, you know too much and you got to make it real simple and engaging at the same time. Yeah, there's a lot of, a lot of work. Yeah. Well, you know, in a way, it's not though, like you, you get all the research, right? You read what, you know, and you just put that aside, right? And then you just talk to people about the product. That's what Gary Halbert used to do. He used to spend weeks just talking to people about the product he was working on, just to see what made their eyes light up, what put them to sleep. Right. And so whether you do that or not, you know, on the page, just talk to people, right? Put the research, put all that stuff aside, you know, it's in your head, you know it, just talk to someone on the page. If you have to, just dictate it, right, into a microphone and then transcribe it. That's a good way sometimes for people to get out of that writing head. But don't, don't overcomplicate it. You don't have to think, like when you're talking to me, right? You're not thinking about, I have to be simple. I have to ask David the right question. I have to not ask questions like this. I have to ask questions. You're just asking me questions, right? You're just talking to me, just like I'm not thinking about what I probably should be, but I'm not thinking about what I'm saying. I'm just talking. So just talk on the page. Don't, don't, don't write. Don't, you know, don't write like you're writing a school paper or something. And, you know, I think this is really, really useful. I'm just taking notes here. Don't, don't write. Just, just talk. I think that is very useful because I think even for myself to today, I sometimes feel like, oh, no, I need to enter that copyright mode. I am like, oh, come on. I need to channel my inner hellbird, my inner Oglevee. But I never thought of like, you know, just like finding a target and just like talking to them like a normal human being. So you always feel, feel like, all right, am I using AI, DA, am I, you know, stacking this offer and am I doing all of these things? Does it flow? Does it sound like all like, you know, or punchy and stuff. But that's not, that's, I think your entire way of writing copy is putting people first. Very, very, very old school yet very like logical and very just common sense. Like go out and talk to people and just at the end of the day, find what connects with people and just put it on a piece of paper. I think that's, that's exactly right. You know, all those things you mentioned, they're good things, right? Do that after you've gotten a first draft on the page. Look at it and go, OK, does it have interest? Does it have action? Does it have, you know, Ada? Does it flow? You know, is the logic good? You know, what would Halbert do? You know, what would Oglevee do? Maybe put a little more news in here. Maybe Halbert would simplify the language a little bit. But, you know, do that after when you're writing, you need to be in a, you know, in a, in a what's called a flow state, right? You need to be just, you know, putting stuff on the page. You don't need to be thinking about what you're doing or not doing or what Oglevee would do or what Oglevee would do or or anything like that. Because, again, you want to tap into that part of you. I mean, you're so articulate when you talk, right? When you describe about, you know, just writing simply and, you know, when you kind of sum up what I've said, right? It's very articulate. How, how, how do people transition away from the bullets towards the clothes and like, how do people design also an attractive offer? You know, I think, I think there's one of the most underrated things that people talk about, you know, offer. So how do you transition towards the offer and what makes a good offer? Well, when you say how to transition, you know, I have a thing about transitions, right? I, I, one of the things I work with with writers is to be able to transition gracefully into the next topic rather than kind of an another thing or, you know, now that we've talked about that, now we're going to talk about your hormone system or something. Right. So when you transition to the offer, you don't, you want it to be like the offer is writing in as the hero to solve these problems. Right. You know, like in a Western hero rides in and the white hat, like, here I am, I'm the hero. I'm going to save this down. The product should be that, right? Because like I've told you all about how hormones are affecting your weight loss. It's why you're not able to lose weight. It's not your fault. It's your hormones. It's not your fault. It's your hormones. It's your hormones. There's a lot of things you could do to balance your hormones. You could eat right. You could exercise. You could go to the doctor and you could, you could, you could get this and that and you could take this herb and that herb and and, you know, you could meditate. And all these things help you have a better hormone balance. Or, you know, now the product rides in as the hero. Right. But you don't always have time to eat better and to exercise and you don't always know which, you know, herbs to take. don't always know which herbs to take. And it's hard to tell which hormones are exactly out of balance. That's why we developed this product called Hormono Balance. And what it does is whatever way your hormones are out of balance, it restores them to balance. The ones that are too much, we make it go back to, we make you make less. The ones that are too little, we make you make more. So it naturally works with your body's own hormone balancing. It reinvigorates your body's own homeostasis or whatever hormone balancing mechanism in order to balance your hormones and make it easy for you to lose weight. Right? That's how you transition to an offer, to a product, right? It's like the hero now. It enables you to do those things to avoid the bad stuff and get the good stuff that you've been talking about up till now. Man, this is incredible. I think the way you describe that the offer writes in as a hero is just a different perspective because most of the time people feel almost this apologetic like alright, cool, let me, I know we're talking about so much, but hey, you know what, there's come time to pay the bills. So let me sell you some shit right now. You know, there's a sense of apology to it. But I learned the way you just, just, yeah, because I think even, even, even a lot of times here in Asia, I think people almost feel apologetic. Yeah, you know, yeah. So, but then the thing that the offer is actually coming in to rescue the day is a brand new way of thinking. Am I right, Joe? So everybody just bear with me. Am I right, Joe? Unless you're a male vain, like he probably sells from the get go in the first sentence or second sentence. Sell from the get go. That is awesome. Wow. Are there any other tips you have for offers and also for guarantees as well? So here, here in Asia, I mean, only folks, people use very, very simple 30 day guarantees. How important is it to play around with offers, guarantees, payment plans and this kind of stuff? So what are your thoughts on this? Well, the offer is certainly important, right? That's what you're selling. And there's so much leverage in the offer. If you offer people exactly what they want, you know, if you offer people what seems like 10 times the value for what they're paying, then it's easy to write copy for that. It's just as important as good copy is to have a great offer. If I can convince you I've got $10 bills and I'm going to sell them to you for $5, that's a great offer. It's pretty easy to write copy for that. You just have to prove they're not counterfeit. You just have to give them a reason why I'm doing this, right? You know, I'm doing this because I had a bet with my, you know, with my partner that I couldn't sell $10 bills for $5. And if you buy these $10 bills, you're going to help me win this bet, right? There's got to be some explanation of why I'm doing, why I'm crazy enough to be, to be doing that, right? Or I got a deal. I was able to buy them for $1. So I'm selling them for $1. I'm actually making money by selling the view for $5, right? So that's all you have to do. So you want your offer to be, you know, I don't like the word, but like a no-brainer, right? Someone doesn't even have to think about it. It's like, yep, at that price, I totally would do this. So you do that, right? By, you know, the things of an offer, it's like what it is, right? What you get, the product itself, are there bonuses? Are there, you know, like bonuses? Sometimes people get things just for the bonuses. And the bonus can just be... What exactly makes a good bonus? Well, there's a couple of things. One is someone really wants it, that would buy the main product, right? And because it, you know, it gives you something the main product didn't give you without making it seem like the main product is incomplete, right? So it, you know, and, you know, if we're selling that course on, you know, let's say it's the thing that's going to make you work on the beach, right? And then maybe since you're going to make a lot of money, so maybe one of the bonuses is how to cut your taxes in half, which is, has a double purpose because not only is that something everyone wants to know how to cut their taxes in half, but it also implies you're going to be making so much money, you're going to need this to cut your taxes in half. So it's kind of a little bit of a way also of reinforcing, you know, the benefit. Wow, that's, that is safe right there. Wow, reinforces the benefits of the main product. Man, man, this is good stuff, man. Oh my God, this is great stuff. How exactly do you work in pricing, David? Because, you know, most of the time here in Asia, people just sell $7, 97, they just look at all these prices at the end of the seven. Is that just the way to go? And do we all have to sell it $2,997, $1,997, $3,997, $97, a section? Well, you're talking about like the seven part of it, or you're talking about just like how did this, just like, just in general, like how exactly do you price? You know, most people don't charge enough. One of the easiest, you know, I got this from Dan Kennedy, one of the easiest things to do as a consultant when you go in is just to say you should raise your prices. And people say, I can't raise my prices, all of the people won't pay anymore. And then you say, just try it, you know, and then they do. And they find that nobody cares that they raise their prices. So that's kind of an easy win, I think. You know, when you raise your prices, it's also good to reinforce the value of what you're giving them. You know, and there's a lot of, we probably know a lot of the ways of doing that, right? You break it down into a daily price, you know, it's like less than a cup of coffee at Starbucks every day. You know, this fee for this seminar that I want you to attend, you know, it may be $1,000, but you know, look at all these people that have made tens of thousands of dollars, or hundreds of thousands of dollars after coming to this seminar. Just one additional client is going to make you, you know, $1,000 a month, you know, reinforcing it with a guarantee, right? Yeah, it costs a thousand, like I, you know, you're charging $1,000. But if you're at all unhappy, if it's not everything I said it is, you get your $1,000 back. If you don't double your money, triple it in terms of more income. So that taking the risk off is a big part, I think, of being able to charge a higher price. Wow, wow, wow. Okay, wow, that's great. I think at the end of the day, I've got a couple more questions. As a copywriter, how exactly do you do like a season copywriter yourself? Or exactly you find good clients and you know, ensure they are pleasant to work with, you know, because that can also link into some of the folks who are listening to this and sometimes they struggle to, you know, find good clients. You know, those that are, those that accept your work will actually mail what you write, instead of trying to edit it and actually pay on time and not just not, you know, terrible people in general to work with. So how exactly do you find this? You know, I was lucky enough to be in the, in two industries that are pretty, at the high end, dependable in terms of working with good people. You know, one of them is news that are publishing and, you know, publishing in general, they're used to paying writers on royalties. You know, companies like Boardroom and Agora. And then the supplement people are also, you know, oriented toward that to a large extent, supplement manufacturers, especially the more, obviously, the more reputable ones. So the being bigger helps that they've worked with other copy writers before, that they have a history of working well with copywriters. You know, this is a, you know, a two hour, another two hour conversation. But you want to, you want people, Dan Rosenfeld used to say, you want people that aren't, that write big checks happily, right? Find those people. They, they're happy when you're making money because they're making money. They're making more money when you make money. That is insane. That is really good. They're right. Big checks happening. Wow. I mean, Boardroom was always like that, right? They were happy to write a big check because they were, they were making, you know, big checks. They didn't, and they could easily have resented it, right? They're people that make a lot of money from people's writings that resent writing big checks. Boardroom never did, right? They were, they were happy to. When you're starting out, or, or, you know, even when you're a little past starting out, you want to be careful, maybe not to depend too much on royalties, like charge enough of a fee that the royalty is kind of gravy. If that makes sense, if you're not sure about the client paying the royalties or getting not sure if the client will mail, I would say also get involved in helping the client to implement, right? And maybe charge more because of that. But, you know, a lot of clients don't implement because they're just busy. They're overwhelmed by things. They don't know exactly what to do. So if you can help them, if you can help them in that implementation, that could be, A, it can help get what you wrote mailed out, or, or distributed or online, and also to help you charge more because you're, you're providing even more service to them. Well, I think everything that you write is, is relevant to what we talked about in terms of writing from yourself, right? Really just talking to people, not writing to people, especially emails, right? Which are, you know, people are used to emails as being a personal thing, right? You get emails from your friends, you get emails from people you work with, you know, like, hey, Melvin, what's going on? And I need this and, you know, or, you know, your, your relatives and your friends. And so, you know, we don't want to get emails that are stilted and that, that sound like they're copyrighted. And we want to get emails that are friendly and that are personal and that are interesting things in our mailboxes. So these same techniques apply, right? To just write, just talk to the person, right? Don't like, you know, like, today I am going to tell you about a very exciting product. But, you know, just, just say, hey, I woke up this morning thinking about, you know, you know, thinking about you and, and, you know, why you're not making as much money as you want or whatever it is, you know what I mean? And, or I was watching Game of Thrones and I was, you know, thinking about what a cutthroat, you know, what, how cutthroat everyone was. And it occurred to me that like, like consulting is kind of like that. It's a really cutthroat business, you know. And, and, you know, I was thinking about how you cut through that, how you, how you, how you be in consulting without being a part of that. And, you know, and, and here's what I was thinking. And, and, you know, like it, here's a way to bypass the cutthroat nature of consulting. But it started out with Game of Thrones and that's cool, because that's people are interested in that and they watch that. And it's, it's how you would talk to someone, right? If you were talking to, talking to someone in person, you'd be like, you know, hell man, I was watching Game of Thrones last night, man, those Lancasters, they're killing all of these people and these people are killing all these people and there's all these plots. And I thought, wow, this is just like consulting, you know, like, like, but it doesn't have to be that way, right? You can stand out above everyone else. You can be the person people just come to, because, because you're above the frame. And I want to, my new course shows you how to do that. So click here for more information or, you know, whatever it is. I am, I am out of questions, Joe. Joe, do you have anything you would love to ask the great David Deutsch? I am asked so many questions. I am out of questions, Joe. I have one. This is just for fun question David. I mean, you have been involved with many projects. I mean, off the top of your mind, is there any one, two, or even three campaigns that you're very, very proud of that you live it so much that you will tell your grandchildren and you hope that it passes on down the legacy as this is my, my legacy? Like, is there any of such? Well, you know, it's funny that you asked that question about legacy because I think one of the things I'm most proud of in a way, at least from a professional point of view, is promoting a book on what's called estate planning. Do you know what that is? That's like, you know, when you die, and it's like, my God, could there be anything that's either more boring or people don't want to deal with, you know, right? Not only is it boring, it's like I don't even want to think about that, right? It's morbid. It's morbid, right? Right? Right? So in a way, it was where I really learned to write bullets, you know, because how do you make a good bullet about estate planning? And, you know, like one of the things was it was this really technical set at section about there are certain things you need to do in your will that must be in the will. You must say this, you must specify this, you know, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, technical, technical, technical stuff. You know, this was in the book, right? Because if you don't, it's possible that, you know, the benefits may not accrue to the people that you are, you know, would, you know, that you have specified that the benefits of your, you know, estate will accrue to. And which, you know, and I translated that into, you know, the little known mistake that can disinherit your heirs. Oh, because that's what it boils down to, right? And that's a way of saying that in the most interesting way possible is how to avoid, you know, accidentally disinheriting your heirs. It's like, wait, what do you mean? I could, like my kids won't get what I'm leaving them because of some little typo in my will. I better, you know, I better find out about this. Wow. You don't do this, what kind of shit happened to you? Yeah, yeah, you know, you know, you really had to get people. So it had stories in it about like, there was some guy that died, a very successful businessman. But when you die, there's a tax on your business, right? And it's passed along to your heirs. And the heirs couldn't afford the tax so that they had to sell this perfectly good business. So, you know, it was a very compelling story. And so it was in a couple of stories like that, when Christopher Reeves, the actor died, there were some problems with, you know, with his will and passing things on. So it was like just this real struggle to make this interesting and, you know, singing and dancing and look at this, this is, you know, and so I'm kind of proud of that. I think the headline was something like everything you always wanted to know about a state, everything your spouse would like you to know about a state planning, but the IRS hopes you never find out. Wow. Yeah. This man, that is incredible. Oh, wow, this is, this is I have never run out of questions on a podcast before. I am out of questions. This is amazing, David. This is incredible. David, final words for folks that are starting out in this, they might not be a David Deutsch, maybe they might write, copy or learn marketing for 30 years and never be a David Deutsch, but where do they begin at what, you know, make, make your wisdom you have. You know, I think you begin by just writing, you know, you begin by writing every day, by copying out ads, successful copy, and by really studying, you know, by being a student, you know, like I said before, you know, it's hard to write better than other people in a way, but anyone can, anyone can know more than the next guy, right? It's easier to know more. It's easier to be more informed about the market you're writing for, about the industry you're writing for, about the prospect that you're writing for, about what came before in terms of ads, right, for writing about weight loss. You could be really smart about it, be the expert in it. And, and as I say, just write every opportunity. If you can't, if you don't have any clients, you know, sell a product on ClickBank, sell, sell your stuff on eBay and practice writing descriptions of it and seeing, like, if you tell a little bit more story in your description, do you sell more on eBay? Because you do, you, they've actually found out that's true. So, you know, practice doing that. There's nothing like actually writing and seeing the results of your writing, right. So, to me, that's a great hack is, is just sell something yourself. And anyone can do that now, as you know, on the internet. Yeah, absolutely.
